title: hijackWriteErrorAndClose use in podman service is unsound
body:
seen once in CI

```
panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference
[signal SIGSEGV: segmentation violation code=0x1 addr=0x40 pc=0x69b8a0]

goroutine 14099 [running]:
net.(*OpError).Unwrap(0x2c31560?)
	/usr/lib/golang/src/net/net.go:499
errors.is({0x1f6eee0?, 0x0?}, {0x1f6e060, 0x2c2cab0}, 0x1)
	/usr/lib/golang/src/errors/wrap.go:63 +0x13c
errors.Is({0x1f6eee0, 0x0}, {0x1f6e060, 0x2c2cab0})
	/usr/lib/golang/src/errors/wrap.go:50 +0x7e
github.com/containers/podman/v6/libpod.hijackWriteError({0x1f6eee0, 0x0}, {0xc00077ee80, 0x40}, 0x0, 0xc0008e78b0)
	/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/libpod/util.go:130 +0x79
github.com/containers/podman/v6/libpod.hijackWriteErrorAndClose({0x1f6eee0?, 0x0?}, {0xc00077ee80, 0x40}, 0x0?, {0x7f404c6261d0, 0xc0005a4b48}, 0xc000057a01?)
	/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/libpod/util.go:152 +0x47
github.com/containers/podman/v6/libpod.attachExecHTTP.func3()
	/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/libpod/oci_conmon_exec_common.go:612 +0x248
created by github.com/containers/podman/v6/libpod.attachExecHTTP in goroutine 14033
	/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/libpod/oci_conmon_exec_common.go:593 +0xab9
```
https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5261985647951872/html/sys-remote-fedora-43-rootless-host.log.html
https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5261985647951872/server_log/podman-server.log

Looking at this the stack trace seems weird, nil panic in errors.Is()?!

However a closer look shows the first error argument with the address of `{0x1f6eee0, 0x0}`, this is the error interface, like any interface it has two addresses, the itable and the data, so somehow the table is set but the data is a nil pointer. Hence once the function is called with the nil pointer it failed.

Now the question how does the interface end up with such an seemingly invalid assignment?

Looking at hijackWriteErrorAndClose() the first error argument is deferredErr which is the return value from the outer function while hijackWriteErrorAndClose() is called within another goroutine.

So my guess the outer function write the value to the deferredErr while the hijackWriteErrorAndClose accessed it from the other goroutine causing unsafe memory access as the interface assignment is not concurrent safe

> This means that races on multiword data structures can lead to inconsistent values not corresponding to a single write. When the values depend on the consistency of internal (pointer, length) or (pointer, type) pairs, as can be the case for interface values, maps, slices, and strings in most Go implementations, such races can in turn lead to arbitrary memory corruption.

https://go.dev/ref/mem
